subject: general-knowledge
description: Ottoman Empire Turkiye MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire was established in the year:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire was established in 1299 by Osman I in northwestern Anatolia.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire was founded by:
mcqs.explanation: Osman I (also known as Osman Gazi) founded the Ottoman Empire in 1299.

mcqs.q: The initial center (first capital) of the Ottoman Empire was:
mcqs.explanation: Bursa was the initial center and first major capital of the Ottoman Empire after its capture in 1326.

mcqs.q: The capital of the Ottoman Empire before Constantinople (1453) was:
mcqs.explanation: Edirne (Adrianople) served as the Ottoman capital from 1369 until the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Ankara (1402) was fought between:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Ankara in 1402 was fought between Timur (Tamerlane) and Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I, resulting in Bayezid's capture.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Kosovo (1389) established Ottoman dominance in:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Kosovo in 1389 established Ottoman dominance in the Balkans and southeastern Europe.

mcqs.q: Sultan Muhammad Fateh (Mehmed II) conquered Constantinople on:
mcqs.explanation: Sultan Mehmed II (Fateh/the Conqueror) conquered Constantinople on May 29, 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire.

mcqs.q: The conquest of Constantinople in 1453 marks the start of the:
mcqs.explanation: The fall of Constantinople in 1453 is often regarded as the event marking the end of the Middle Ages and the start of the Modern Era.

mcqs.q: After conquering Constantinople, the Ottomans renamed it to:
mcqs.explanation: Constantinople was gradually renamed Istanbul after the Ottoman conquest, becoming the empire's grand capital.

mcqs.q: The Caliphate was established in the Ottoman Empire after defeating the Mamluks in:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Caliphate was established in 1517 after Sultan Selim I defeated the Mamluk Sultanate and assumed the title of Caliph.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Chaldiran (1514) was fought against the:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Chaldiran in 1514 was fought between Sultan Selim I and the Safavid Shah Ismail I of Persia.

mcqs.q: The tenure of Suleiman the Magnificent was from:
mcqs.explanation: Suleiman the Magnificent ruled the Ottoman Empire from 1520 to 1566, its longest and most glorious era.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire entered its classical golden age under:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire reached its peak under Suleiman the Magnificent, expanding across three continents.

mcqs.q: The primary elite military unit of Christian boys converted to Islam were the:
mcqs.explanation: The Janissaries were an elite military corps formed from Christian boys through the devshirme system, converted to Islam and trained as soldiers.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Lepanto (1571) was a major naval defeat for the Ottomans against:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Lepanto in 1571 was a major naval defeat for the Ottomans at the hands of the Holy League (Spain, Venice, and the Papacy).

mcqs.q: Hungary remained under Ottoman control for:
mcqs.explanation: Hungary was under Ottoman rule from 1541 to 1699, a period of 158 years.

mcqs.q: The period during which Hungary was under Ottoman rule was:
mcqs.explanation: Hungary was under Ottoman control from 1541 to 1699, ending with the Treaty of Karlowitz.

mcqs.q: The Crimean War began on:
mcqs.explanation: The Crimean War began on October 5, 1853, primarily between Russia and the Ottoman Empire (allied with Britain and France).

mcqs.q: The Crimean War involved which Russian and Ottoman leaders?
options: Tsar Nicholas I and Sultan Abdulmejid I
Tsar Alexander II and Sultan Mehmed V
Tsar Alexander I and Sultan Mahmud II
Tsar Nicholas II and Sultan Abdul Hamid II
mcqs.explanation: The Crimean War involved Tsar Nicholas I of Russia against Sultan Abdulmejid I of the Ottoman Empire.

mcqs.q: The primary result of the Crimean War for the Ottomans was:
mcqs.explanation: The Crimean War led to a rise of Ottoman nationalism and efforts to modernize and reform the empire.

mcqs.q: Turkey became known as the 'Sick Man of Europe' around:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire was famously called the 'Sick Man of Europe' around 1876 due to its declining power and territorial losses.

mcqs.q: Prior to the 19th century, the Ottoman Empire's main rival was:
mcqs.explanation: Russia was the Ottoman Empire's principal rival, with multiple Russo-Turkish wars fought over centuries.

mcqs.q: The military commander of Sultan Abdul Hamid II was:
mcqs.explanation: Hassan Pasha served as the military commander under Sultan Abdulhamid II.

mcqs.q: Sultan Abdulhamid II promoted pan-Islamic ideology with authoritarian centralization during:
mcqs.explanation: Sultan Abdulhamid II ruled from 1876 to 1909, promoting pan-Islamic ideology amid crises (1876-1909).

mcqs.q: The Tanzimat reforms sought to modernize the Ottoman Empire through:
mcqs.explanation: The Tanzimat reforms (1839-1876) sought legal and administrative modernization amid imperial pressures.

mcqs.q: The Young Turk Revolution took place in:
mcqs.explanation: The Young Turk Revolution of 1908 restored the Ottoman constitution and aimed at imperial reform.

mcqs.q: Sultan Abdulhamid II was weakened after the:
mcqs.explanation: Sultan Abdulhamid II was weakened and later deposed after the Young Turk Revolution of 1908.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire officially entered World War I in:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire entered WWI in 1914, aligning with the Central Powers (Germany and Austria-Hungary).

mcqs.q: The Gallipoli Campaign (1915-1916) saw Ottoman forces successfully defend against:
mcqs.explanation: The Gallipoli Campaign saw Ottoman forces, led partly by Mustafa Kemal, successfully repel the Allied invasion at the Dardanelles.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Sèvres was significant because it led to the:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Sèvres (1920) dismembered the Ottoman Empire, but was later replaced by the Treaty of Lausanne.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Sultanate was abolished on:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Sultanate was abolished on November 1, 1922 by the Grand National Assembly of Turkey.

mcqs.q: The last Sultan of the Ottoman Sultanate was:
mcqs.explanation: Mehmed VI (Vahideddin) was the last Sultan of the Ottoman Empire, ruling from 1918 to 1922.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Caliphate was abolished on:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Caliphate was abolished on March 3, 1924 by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk.

mcqs.q: The last Caliph of the Ottoman Caliphate was:
mcqs.explanation: Abdulmejid II was the last Caliph of the Ottoman Caliphate, serving from 1922 until abolition in 1924.

mcqs.q: The institution of Caliphate was abolished by:
mcqs.explanation: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk abolished the Ottoman Caliphate in 1924 as part of his secularization reforms.

mcqs.q: The civil government of Turkey (Republic) was formed in:
mcqs.explanation: The Republic of Turkey was proclaimed on October 29, 1923 with Mustafa Kemal Atatürk as its first president.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Lausanne (1923) served as the basis to form:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Lausanne (1923) replaced the Treaty of Sèvres and recognized the sovereignty of the new Republic of Turkey.

mcqs.q: Hagia Sophia was originally built by the:
mcqs.explanation: Hagia Sophia was built by the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I in 537 AD as a Christian cathedral in Constantinople.

mcqs.q: Hagia Sophia is located in:
mcqs.explanation: Hagia Sophia is located in Istanbul, Turkey. It served as a cathedral, mosque, museum, and is now a mosque again.

mcqs.q: The Sultan Ahmed Mosque of Istanbul is also called the:
mcqs.explanation: The Sultan Ahmed Mosque, built in the early 17th century, is popularly known as the Blue Mosque due to its blue tile interior.

mcqs.q: The political party of President Erdoğan is the:
mcqs.explanation: Recep Tayyip Erdoğan leads the Justice and Development Party (AKP), which he co-founded in 2001.

mcqs.q: President Erdoğan was fond of which sport in his young age?
mcqs.explanation: Recep Tayyip Erdoğan was a passionate footballer in his youth and played semi-professional football.

mcqs.q: Which foreign leader addressed the Parliament of Pakistan for the third time?
mcqs.explanation: Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan addressed the Parliament of Pakistan for the third time, reflecting strong Pakistan-Turkey ties.

mcqs.q: The writer of Turkish drama 'Diriliş: Ertuğrul' is:
mcqs.explanation: Mehmet Bozdağ is the creator and writer of the popular Turkish historical drama Diriliş: Ertuğrul.

mcqs.q: The last holder of the Prime Minister post in Turkey was:
mcqs.explanation: Binali Yıldırım was the last Prime Minister of Turkey before the post was abolished in 2018.

mcqs.q: The post of Prime Minister of Turkey was abolished in:
mcqs.explanation: Turkey abolished the post of Prime Minister in 2018 after a constitutional referendum shifted to a presidential system.

mcqs.q: Turkey officially changed its name to Türkiye on:
mcqs.explanation: Turkey officially changed its internationally recognized name to Türkiye on June 1, 2022 at the United Nations.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire at its peak spanned across:
mcqs.explanation: At its height under Suleiman the Magnificent, the Ottoman Empire spanned Europe, Asia, and Africa.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) marked the first major Ottoman territorial losses to:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) marked the first major Ottoman territorial losses, ceding Hungary to Austria and other lands.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire's 'millet system' was a form of:
mcqs.explanation: The millet system allowed religious communities (Christians, Jews) a degree of self-governance under Ottoman rule.

mcqs.q: The devshirme system involved:
options: Recruiting Christian boys for military and administrative service
mcqs.explanation: The devshirme system recruited Christian boys from the Balkans, converting them to Islam and training them as Janissaries or administrators.

mcqs.q: Sultan Selim I (Selim the Grim) conquered Egypt and assumed the Caliphate in:
mcqs.explanation: Sultan Selim I defeated the Mamluks and conquered Egypt in 1517, assuming the title of Caliph.

mcqs.q: The Siege of Vienna (1683) was the Ottoman Empire's last major attempt to:
mcqs.explanation: The Siege of Vienna in 1683 was the Ottomans' last major attempt to expand into Central Europe, ending in defeat.

mcqs.q: Göbekli Tepe, the world's oldest known temple complex (c. 9600 BCE), is located in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Göbekli Tepe, dating to c. 9600 BCE, is located in southeastern Türkiye and is the world's oldest known temple complex.

mcqs.q: Ephesus, an ancient Greek city famous for the Temple of Artemis, is in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Ephesus is an ancient Greek city in present-day Türkiye, famous for the Temple of Artemis (one of the Seven Wonders).

mcqs.q: Troy, an ancient city associated with the Trojan War, is located in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Troy is located in present-day Türkiye (near Çanakkale), associated with the legendary Trojan War.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman-Habsburg rivalry dominated European politics for nearly:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman-Habsburg rivalry dominated European politics from the 15th to 18th centuries, spanning nearly three centuries.

mcqs.q: The Balkan Wars (1912-1913) weakened the Ottoman Empire and redrew borders in:
mcqs.explanation: The Balkan Wars (1912-1913) saw the Ottoman Empire lose most of its European territories to Balkan states.

mcqs.q: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk served as the first President of Turkey from:
mcqs.explanation: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk served as the first President of the Republic of Turkey from 1923 until his death in 1938.

mcqs.q: Atatürk's reforms included replacing the Arabic script with:
mcqs.explanation: Atatürk replaced the Arabic script with the Latin alphabet in 1928 as part of his modernization and secularization reforms.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire was dissolved after which war?
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman Empire was dissolved after World War I, with the Treaty of Sèvres (1920) and later the Treaty of Lausanne (1923).

mcqs.q: The Khilafat Movement in British India sought to preserve the Ottoman Caliphate, mobilizing Indian Muslims during:
mcqs.explanation: The Khilafat Movement (1919-1924) in British India sought to preserve the Ottoman Caliphate, mobilizing Indian Muslims.

mcqs.q: Turkey joined NATO as a member in:
mcqs.explanation: Turkey joined NATO in 1952, becoming a key Western alliance member on the southern flank during the Cold War.

mcqs.q: The 2016 coup attempt in Turkey was directed against:
mcqs.explanation: The 2016 failed coup attempt was directed against President Erdoğan's government, leading to widespread purges and state of emergency.

mcqs.q: Turkey's application to join the European Union has been pending since:
mcqs.explanation: Turkey formally applied for EU membership in 1987, with accession negotiations beginning in 2005 but remaining stalled.

mcqs.q: The Bosphorus Strait, connecting the Black Sea to the Sea of Marmara, is located in:
mcqs.explanation: The Bosphorus Strait runs through Istanbul, Türkiye, connecting the Black Sea to the Sea of Marmara and dividing Europe from Asia.

mcqs.q: The Ottoman Empire's legal system was based on:
mcqs.explanation: The Ottoman legal system combined Sharia (Islamic law) with Kanun (secular law decreed by the Sultan) for governance.

mcqs.q: Suleiman the Magnificent was known in the Ottoman Empire as:
mcqs.explanation: Within the Ottoman Empire, Suleiman was known as 'Kanuni' (the Lawgiver) for his comprehensive reform of Ottoman law.

mcqs.q: The capitulations granted by the Ottoman Empire gave special privileges to:
mcqs.explanation: The capitulations were trade and legal privileges granted to European powers, which increasingly undermined Ottoman sovereignty.

mcqs.q: The Committee of Union and Progress (CUP) was the political organization behind the:
mcqs.explanation: The Committee of Union and Progress (CUP) was the political organization that led the Young Turk Revolution of 1908.

mcqs.q: The Topkapi Palace in Istanbul served as the primary residence of Ottoman sultans for approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The Topkapi Palace served as the primary residence and administrative center of Ottoman sultans for about 400 years (1465-1856).

mcqs.q: The Treaty of San Stefano (1878) forced the Ottoman Empire to cede territory to:
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of San Stefano (1878) after the Russo-Turkish War forced the Ottomans to cede territory, reshaping the Balkans.
